one_line_takeaway: Across 16 LLMs, 5,000 balanced samples, and 25 CWEs, LLMs are only modestly effective (62.8% avg accuracy) at vulnerability detection, don't improve with scale, and lag static analysis overall — but reliably identify sources/sinks even when their final verdict is wrong.
key_finding: LLMs show only modest end-to-end vulnerability-detection ability (best-per-dataset average 62.8% accuracy, 0.71 F1), do not improve with model scale (Qwen-2.5-14B/32B beat GPT-4 on real-world data), and trail CodeQL overall on synthetic benchmarks — yet the paper's novel CWE-DF prompt lifts real-world F1 by up to 0.18 and LLMs reliably identify correct sources/sinks/sanitizers (17-18/20 samples) even when their final verdict is wrong.
core_contribution: An empirical measurement study (Khare, Dutta, Li, et al., UPenn/Cornell) of how well 16 pre-trained LLMs detect known security vulnerabilities in source code. The paper's own contribution is the study itself: it curates a balanced 5,000-sample benchmark (1,000 each drawn from five datasets), evaluates 16 LLMs across two languages (C/C++ and Java) and 25 CWE classes under three prompting strategies, and — for the first time in this line of work per its own Table II — compares LLMs head-to-head with both a static-analysis tool (CodeQL) and two prior deep-learning detectors (DeepDFA, LineVul). It also contributes a novel "Dataflow analysis-based" (CWE-DF) prompt that asks the model to simulate a source-sink-sanitizer analysis before predicting. Note: DeepDFA, LineVul, and CodeQL are cited prior/external tools used as baselines, NOT contributions of this paper; the CWE-DF prompt and the comparative study are what the paper itself introduces.
threat_model: This is a defensive evaluation/measurement study rather than an attack paper: it measures how effectively LLMs perform the security-relevant task of detecting known software vulnerabilities (buffer overflows, injection, use-after-free, etc.) in C/C++ and Java code, and benchmarks them against static-analysis (CodeQL) and deep-learning detectors. There is no adversary or attacker model — the "threat" is undetected vulnerabilities in real software, and the paper assesses whether LLM-based detectors are trustworthy/deployable for finding them.
limitations: By the paper's own framing: (1) snippets over 2048 tokens and multi-file benchmarks are excluded, so results only reflect intra-snippet reasoning and the authors explicitly attribute poor real-world C/C++ performance to missing global context (pointers/structs); (2) CodeQL comparison is restricted to the three synthetic datasets because CodeQL needs full project builds, so no static-analysis comparison exists on real-world code; (3) the qualitative findings on sources/sinks/sanitizers rest on small manually inspected subsets (~20 samples each), not the full 5,000; (4) modest overall accuracy (62.8%) and the synthetic-vs-real gap lead the authors to conclude LLMs "might not be suitable for real-world vulnerability detection yet"; (5) closed-model versions (gpt-4-0613, gpt-3.5-turbo-0613) are fixed snapshots. The paper reports no true attack/robustness evaluation of the detectors themselves.

datasets_used: BigVul (C/C++, used only for training DL baselines)
5,000 total balanced samples across 25 MITRE Top-25 CWE classes
quantitative_results: Best model/prompt per dataset: 62.8% average accuracy, 0.71 average F1
Highest single result: Llama-3.1-70B (CWE prompt) 76% accuracy on Juliet Java
Synthetic datasets average 10.5% higher best-accuracy than real-world datasets
CWE-DF prompt improves F1 by up to 0.18 (CVEFixes C/C++) and 0.14 (CVEFixes Java) over other prompts
CodeQL vs best LLM F1: Juliet Java 0.94 vs 0.79; OWASP 0.72 vs 0.65; Juliet C/C++ 0.61 vs 0.72 (Qwen-2.5-14B beats CodeQL by 0.11)
CodeQL beats Llama-3.1-70B on Integer Overflow (CWE-190) by 11% (Juliet Java) and 21% (Juliet C/C++) accuracy
DSCoder-7B beats CodeQL on NULL Pointer Dereference (CWE-476) by 12% accuracy on Juliet Java; +24% on Use-After-Free CWE-416 Juliet C/C++
Llama-3.1-70B on CWE-78: 64% (CVEFixes Java) and 78% (CVEFixes C/C++) accuracy
GPT-4/Llama-3.1-8B on CVEFixes Java: up to 89% (CWE-787) and 84% (CWE-125) accuracy, but <53% on same CWEs in CVEFixes C/C++

DeepDFA/LineVul reach >98-99% accuracy in-distribution on BigVul but drop to 51%/59% accuracy on CVEFixes C/C++ cross-dataset; Qwen-2.5-32B 56% acc / 0.65 F1 (F1 +0.42 over DeepDFA, +0.04 over LineVul)
Basic prompt predicts an incorrect CWE in >60% of Java and >53% of C/C++ samples (avg across 4 models)
CWE-DF correctly identifies sources/sinks in 18/20 samples and sources/sinks/sanitizers in 17/20 even when final prediction is wrong
baselines_compared: DeepDFA (GNN over CFG + dataflow, deep learning)
Cross-comparison among the 16 LLMs themselves (GPT-4, GPT-3.5, Gemini-1.5-Flash, CodeLlama 7/13/34B, Llama-3.1 8/70B, Codestral-22B, DeepSeekCoder 7/15/33B, Qwen-2.5 and Qwen-2.5-Coder 1.5-32B)
